chunks.page_summary: This page provides an overview and initial steps for reading Modbus registers from Rheonics Smart Module Electronics (SME) using the KEPServerEX 6 industrial connectivity platform.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Reading Modbus Registers from a Rheonics SME Using KEPserverEX 6

Modified on: Thu, 2 Jan, 2025 at 8:51 PM

1. Overview
KEPServerEX is an industrial connectivity platform that bridges industrial devices with control systems. For Rheonics viscosity and density sensors, the included Smart Module Electronics (SME) (https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/) enables communication across major protocols, including Modbus, facilitating the integration with customer hardware and needs. This article will walk through setting up KEPServerEX 6 to read Modbus input registers from Rheonics sensors.
KEPSeverEX 6 can be downloaded from its official website (https://www.kepserverexopc.com/) as a 100% functional demo.

Figure 1. KEPServer Diagram. Image Sourced from Kepware Website

2. Rheonics SME: Modbus TCP
The Rheonics SME offers Modbus TCP over an Ethernet interface. The Modbus protocol provides client/server communication between devices connected on an Ethernet network. As shown in Figure 2, an SME (Modbus Server) connected to a network with several Modbus clients is able to send process and
key_facts: KEPServerEX is an industrial connectivity platform designed to bridge industrial devices with control systems.
Rheonics viscosity and density sensors utilize Smart Module Electronics (SME) for communication across major protocols, including Modbus.
The document aims to guide users through setting up KEPServerEX 6 to read Modbus input registers from Rheonics sensors.
A 100% functional demo of KEPServerEX 6 is available for download from its official website (https://www.kepserverexopc.com/).
Rheonics SME supports Modbus TCP communication via an Ethernet interface.
The Modbus protocol enables client/server communication for devices on an Ethernet network.
An SME configured as a Modbus Server can transmit process data to multiple Modbus clients.

chunks.page_summary: This page details the setup of Modbus TCP communication for Rheonics SME devices, including a network diagram and instructions for configuring the device and network using RCP software via USB or Ethernet.
chunks.extracted_text:
status data upon request to multiple clients such as PLCs, HMIs, and more.

Figure 2. Modbus TCP Communication illustrates a network setup where a Rheonics SME-004 device, acting as a Modbus TCP Server, is connected via an Ethernet switch to two Modbus TCP Clients. Client 1 is depicted as a generic laptop, and Client 2 is a server rack running KEPserverEx. The Rheonics SME-004 device itself has labels for power inputs (24 V dc +, gnd -), Modbus (RS485), status indicators (power, sensor, comm. C, data, display, SD-card, reset), and output/input terminals (ch-1 out-put, loop-t, gnd, ch-1 in+, ch-2 in+, dts +, dts -).

KEPServer can be a viable solution for building a centralized data management system, as well as providing protocol conversion from Modbus to OPC, MQTT, or other industry-standard protocols.

3. Setting up the Ethernet Network

The first step is to successfully connect the Rheonics SME to the KEPserver Ethernet network. This involves not only creating a physical connection but also ensuring proper communication by configuring both devices on the same subnet. The exact network topology will depend on the use case and customer requirements.

3.1 Network Configuration on Rheonics SME

The Rheonics SME can be configured with the RCP software; to aid the user, Rheonics Support Portal - RCP (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000211411) provides several articles about minimum requirements, installing the software and most importantly: Running the RCP Program (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297177-running-the-rcp-program); there are two options for this: USB or Ethernet communication, both of which are described on the article.

Regardless of the communication method used, the network configuration remains the same. The detailed configuration procedure can be found at: Connecting to your SME using ethernet (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000397449-verifying-the-network-setup-on-the-pc-where-the-rcp-software-is-installed)
key_facts: KEPServer provides centralized data management and protocol conversion from Modbus to OPC, MQTT, or other industry-standard protocols.
Successful connection of the Rheonics SME to the KEPserver Ethernet network requires both a physical connection and proper configuration of devices on the same subnet.
The network topology for Rheonics SME will vary based on specific use case and customer requirements.
The Rheonics SME is configurable using the RCP software.
The Rheonics Support Portal - RCP offers resources including minimum requirements, software installation, and instructions for running the RCP Program.
Configuration of the Rheonics SME can be done via USB or Ethernet communication.
The network configuration remains consistent regardless of the chosen communication method (USB or Ethernet).
A detailed procedure for connecting to the SME via Ethernet and verifying network setup is available on the Rheonics support portal.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions for configuring network settings and creating a Modbus TCP/IP Ethernet channel within KEPServerEX software for connection with an SME (Smart Manufacturing Environment) device.
chunks.extracted_text:
3.2 Network Configuration on KEPServer

The KEPServer must be on the same local network as the SME. This procedure is well documented on the first part of the following article: Connecting to your SME using ethernet (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000397449-verifying-the-network-setup-on-the-pc-where-the-rcp-software-is-installed)

4. KEPServerEX Settings

In KEPServerEX, setting up a Modbus connection involves several steps. It begins by creating a communication channel that defines the data path. Next, a Modbus device is added to this channel with the appropriate network settings. Finally, tags are configured to read specific Input Registers from the SME which then can be used for data logging, monitoring, or integrating with other applications.

Usually, the default settings in KEPServerEX are correct; the general recommendation is that if the user is not sure about any parameters or settings, it's best to leave them at their default values. If needed, the settings can be modified later.

4.1 Creating a Modbus Channel

Create a New KEPServerEX project or open an existing one. Start by creating a Modbus Channel, see Figure 3. Select the option Modbus TCP/IP Ethernet.
key_facts: The KEPServer must be on the same local network as the SME.
Setting up a Modbus connection in KEPServerEX involves creating a communication channel, adding a Modbus device, and configuring tags.
Tags are configured to read specific Input Registers from the SME.
Configured tags can be used for data logging, monitoring, or integrating with other applications.
Default settings in KEPServerEX are generally correct, and it is recommended to leave them at their default values if unsure.
KEPServerEX settings can be modified later if needed.
To create a Modbus Channel, one must create a new KEPServerEX project or open an existing one.
The 'Modbus TCP/IP Ethernet' option must be selected when creating a channel.

Create as many channels as needed but take into account that multiple devices can also be added to a single channel. If the user needs to retrieve data from several Rheonics sensors, they can all be added to the same channel if they share compatible communication settings.

4.2 Adding a New Device: SME

Add a new device, which in this example will be named "SME." Choose a device name that makes sense, such as the sensor's serial number, especially if multiple devices will be added.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions and critical information regarding the configuration of Modbus Device IDs and Modbus Port settings for Rheonics SME devices.
chunks.extracted_text:
Device IDs are specified as <HOST>.XXX. For this case HOST is the SME's IP address. XXX is the Modbus Bridge Index, a number from 0 to 255. Since KEPserver and the SME are directly connected via Ethernet, the Modbus Bridge Index should be set to 0.

[Add Device Wizard UI]
Add Device Wizard
Specify the device's driver-specific station or node.
ID:
<192.168.3.6>.0
Next
Cancel

Figure 8. Device Modbus ID

For Rheonics SME, the Modbus Port is 502. It cannot be changed by the user. If a different port is needed, contact Rheonics Support.
key_facts: Device IDs are specified as <HOST>.XXX, where HOST is the SME's IP address and XXX is the Modbus Bridge Index.
The Modbus Bridge Index is a number that can range from 0 to 255.
If KEPserver and the SME are directly connected via Ethernet, the Modbus Bridge Index must be set to 0.
The Modbus Port for Rheonics SME is fixed at 502.
The Modbus Port cannot be changed by the user.
diagram_labels: Specify the device's driver-specific station or node.
warnings_notes: For Rheonics SME, the Modbus Port is 502. It cannot be changed by the user.
If a different port is needed, contact Rheonics Support.

chunks.page_summary: This page displays a step in an 'Add Device Wizard' for configuring Modbus communication settings, including port number, IP protocol, and socket timeout behavior, specifically noting defaults for Rheonics Sensors.
chunks.extracted_text:
Add Device Wizard
Specify the port number that the remote device is configured to use for solicited requests. The Modbus Ethernet driver uses this port number when making solicited requests to a device.
Port:
502
Indicate whether the driver should use User Datagram Protocol (UDP) or Transfer Control Protocol (TCP). The Modbus client and server settings must match.
IP Protocol:
TCP/IP
Enable the driver to close a TCP socket connection if a device does not respond within the timeout. If disabled, the same socket is used until an error occurs, the physical device closes the socket, or the driver shuts down.
Close Socket on Timeout:
Enable
Next
Cancel
Figure 9. Default Port 502 for Modbus
The Modbus Data Access and Data Encoding Settings configurations define how data is accessed and encoded for communication with Modbus devices. These settings define address conventions, bit-level access, supported Modbus functions, and byte order. The options for Figure 10 and Figure 11 are the default values and will work for Rheonics Sensors.
key_facts: The Modbus Ethernet driver uses a specified port number for solicited requests to a device.
The Modbus client and server settings for IP Protocol (UDP or TCP) must match.
The driver can be configured to close a TCP socket connection if a device does not respond within a timeout.
Modbus Data Access and Data Encoding Settings define how data is accessed and encoded for communication with Modbus devices.
These settings cover address conventions, bit-level access, supported Modbus functions, and byte order.
The depicted options (and those for Figures 10 and 11, though not shown) are default values and are compatible with Rheonics Sensors.
warnings_notes: The Modbus client and server settings for IP Protocol must match.
If 'Close Socket on Timeout' is disabled, the same socket is used until an error occurs, the physical device closes the socket, or the driver shuts down.

chunks.page_summary: This page from an 'Add Device Wizard' allows users to configure Modbus data access settings, including addressing conventions and specific Modbus function support for a connected device.
chunks.extracted_text:
Add Device Wizard

Specify if the address numbering convention for the device starts at zero or one. Addresses have one subtracted when frames are constructed to communicate with a Modbus device. If the device doesn't follow this convention, choose Disable.
Zero-Based Addressing:
Enable

Specify if the first bit in a register address begins at zero or one for memory types that allow bits within words to be referenced as a Boolean.
Zero-Based Bit Addressing:
Enable

Enable if the device supports holding register bit access to manipulate only the bit of interest in a single command (as opposed to performing a Read/Modify/Write operation to manipulate a single bit).
Holding Register Bit Writes:
Enable

Enable if the device can use Modbus functions 06 and 16 for single register writes. If the device only supports Modbus function 16, disable.
Modbus Function 06:
Enable

Enable if the device can use Modbus functions 05 and 15 for single coil writes. If the device only supports Modbus function 15, disable.
Modbus Function 05:
Enable

Next
Cancel

Figure 10. Data Access Settings
key_facts: Zero-Based Addressing determines if a device's address numbering convention starts at zero or one, affecting how Modbus frames are constructed.
If a Modbus device does not adhere to the zero-based addressing convention, the 'Zero-Based Addressing' setting should be disabled.
Zero-Based Bit Addressing specifies whether the first bit in a register address starts at zero or one for memory types that support Boolean bit referencing.
The 'Holding Register Bit Writes' setting should be enabled if the device allows manipulating individual bits within a holding register via a single Modbus command.
Modbus Function 06 should be enabled if the device supports both Modbus functions 06 and 16 for single register writes.
If the Modbus device exclusively supports Modbus function 16 for single register writes, Modbus Function 06 should be disabled.
Modbus Function 05 should be enabled if the device supports both Modbus functions 05 and 15 for single coil writes.
If the Modbus device exclusively supports Modbus function 15 for single coil writes, Modbus Function 05 should be disabled.
warnings_notes: If the device doesn't follow the zero-based addressing convention, choose Disable.
If the device only supports Modbus function 16 for single register writes, disable Modbus Function 06.
If the device only supports Modbus function 15 for single coil writes, disable Modbus Function 05.

chunks.page_summary: This page displays a software wizard interface for configuring data encoding settings for a new device, including Modbus byte order, data type conventions, and bit order.
chunks.extracted_text:
<- Add Device Wizard

Select Enable to use Modbus byte ordering for Modbus-compatible devices or Disable to use Intel byte ordering.
Modbus Byte Order: Enable [dropdown arrow] [question mark icon]

Indicate if 32-bit data types use the convention of first word low, as in Modicon Modsoft programming software. If disabled, the first word is assumed high.
First Word Low: Enable [dropdown arrow] [question mark icon]

Indicate if 64-bit data types use the convention of first DWord low. If disabled, the first DWord is assumed high.
First DWord Low: Enable [dropdown arrow] [question mark icon]

Indicate if the bit order should be reversed on register reads and writes to follow Modicon Modsoft programming software convention. If enabled, the lowest bit becomes the Most Significant Bit (MSB).
Modicon Bit Order: Disable [dropdown arrow] [question mark icon]

Encode/decode Long and DWORD data types as double-precision 64-bit unsigned decimal values. For values read above the specified range, behavior is undefined.
Treat Longs as Decimals: Disable [dropdown arrow] [question mark icon]

[Next button] [Cancel button]

Figure 11. Data Encoding Settings

Click Finish to end the New Device configuration.
key_facts: The page is part of an 'Add Device Wizard' used for configuring a new device.
It allows users to select Modbus byte ordering for Modbus-compatible devices or Intel byte ordering.
Users can define the convention for 32-bit data types, specifically if the first word is low (Modicon Modsoft) or high.
Users can define the convention for 64-bit data types, specifically if the first DWord is low or high.
An option exists to reverse the bit order on register reads and writes to comply with Modicon Modsoft programming software, making the lowest bit the Most Significant Bit (MSB).
Long and DWORD data types are encoded/decoded as double-precision 64-bit unsigned decimal values.
The process concludes by clicking 'Finish' to complete the new device configuration.
warnings_notes: For values read above the specified range when treating Longs as Decimals, behavior is undefined.

4.3 Creating Data Tags

Once the device has been added, the user can create as many tags as Input Registers needed. The Input Registers available are shown on Rheonics Support Portal: Modbus TCP - Input Registers (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297664-modbus-tcp-input-registers). For this example, Median Viscosity (Float32) will be selected.
key_facts: The page displays the final steps of configuring a device using an 'Add Device Wizard'.
The device is configured to use 'Modbus TCP/IP Ethernet' as its driver.
Communication timeouts are set to 3 seconds for connection, 1000 milliseconds for requests, with 3 attempts before timeout.
Data collection is enabled, and the device is not simulated.
Tag generation on device startup is configured to 'Do Not Generate on Startup', and on duplicate tag to 'Delete on Create'.
Users can create data tags from 'Input Registers' found on the Rheonics Support Portal.
An example of a selected data tag is 'Median Viscosity (Float32)'.

It's possible to import and export CSV files to manage predefined tags. By pressing Export, users can save the currently created tags as a backup. Pressing Import allows users to add new tags from the imported file.
key_facts: Data tags are not strictly required but make data browsable by OPC clients.
Rheonics input registers primarily use two data types: Float32 and SInt16.
When configuring a Float32 data type, 'Float' should be selected in the software.
When configuring an SInt16 data type, 'Word' should be selected in the software.
The example configuration for Viscosity uses Address 300040, Float data type, Read Only client access, and a 100 ms scan rate.
The software allows for importing and exporting CSV files to manage predefined tags.
Exporting tags provides a backup mechanism for existing tag configurations.
Importing tags enables users to add new tag definitions from a file.

warnings_notes: Tags are not required, but are browsable by OPC clients.

chunks.page_summary: This page describes how to use KEPserverEX Quick Client for verifying sensor tag configurations and provides a troubleshooting guide for Rheonics sensors' common electronics, probe, and communication issues.
chunks.extracted_text:
4.4 QuickClient

The Quick Client in KEPserverEX is a tool used to test and monitor the tags that the user has configured. It allows users to verify if the setup process was successful. To access it, select the Quick Client option from the navigation bar. A new window will open with several options.

When clicking <Channel Name>.<Device>, the tags that were created will be displayed. If live data for the configured registers appears and is continuously updating, it indicates that the configuration is correct and that the data type and input registers are properly set up.

5. Troubleshooting

This section addresses common issues with input registers, connectivity, and Modbus configuration. Users can follow these steps to identify and resolve typical Ethernet/Modbus communication issues with Rheonics Sensors.

Troubleshooting Guide:
Electronics Issues:
  - Problem: No LED lights, display, or output signals
    - Solution: 1. Check power supply and cabling
  - Problem: Viscosity is not stable
    - Solution: 2. The sensor is not fully submerged in the liquid

Probe Issues:
  - Problem: NaN values are displayed on the RCP software
    - Solution: 1. Check wiring from the probe to SME is correct.
              2. Measure the internal resistance of the probe. Contact Rheonics support team for detailed instructions.

Communication Issues:
  - (No specific issues or solutions listed under this category on the page)
key_facts: The Quick Client in KEPserverEX is a tool for testing and monitoring configured tags to verify successful setup.
Live and continuously updating data in KEPserverEX Quick Client indicates a correct configuration of data types and input registers.
The troubleshooting section helps resolve common Ethernet/Modbus communication issues with Rheonics Sensors.
An unstable viscosity reading can be caused by the sensor not being fully submerged in the liquid.
NaN values displayed on the RCP software for Rheonics sensors may be due to incorrect probe-to-SME wiring or issues with the probe's internal resistance.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides troubleshooting guidance for common issues encountered with Rheonics viscometers and density sensors, followed by a list of external references for further information.
chunks.extracted_text:
No data is visible
  1. Try to reset the SME and/or restart the devices
  2. Try to use the ping command to get a response from either the KEPServer or SME.
  3. Verify there is no duplicated address in the network
  4. Check all settings are correct with the RCP
  5. Check that communication settings (protocol, port 502, network adapter, and device IP) are correct on KEPServer side
  6. Look for hardware malfunction, broken cable or bad Ethernet Port

Data issues

The measured value is different from my reference standards
  1. If the fluid is non-Newtonian, the viscosity readings may vary due to the fluid's characteristics (shear rate). For more information: Non-Newtonian fluid viscosity measurement with inline viscometer SRV (https://rheonics.com/measurement-of-viscosity-of-non-newtonian-fluids-with-rheonics-srv-sensors/)

Wrong parameter reading
  1. Verify Modbus register address. It might be off due to 0/1 indexing.
  2. Verify the data type matches the expected configuration.

Sensor Status does not match any bit from the sensor status table
  1. Sensor status byte is swapped.

Density value displayed is always constant
  1. The density value on the SRV is constant value set with the RCP software.

6. References

1. Rheonics, "SRV Inline Viscometer." [Online]. Available: Rheonics SRV » inline online Viscometer for fluid viscosity monitoring (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/). [Accessed: Nov. 27, 2024].

2. Rheonics, "SRD Inline Density Meter." [Online]. Available: Rheonics SRD » Density Meter inline online specific gravity concentration. (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/). [Accessed: Nov. 27, 2024].

3. Rheonics, "Electronics and Communication." [Online]. Available: Electronics and Communication » rheonics :: viscometer and density meter (https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/). [Accessed: Nov. 27, 2024].

4. Rheonics, "Modbus TCP Input Registers." [Online]. Available: Modbus TCP - Input Registers (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297664-modbus-tcp-input-registers). [Accessed: Nov. 27, 2024].
key_facts: Troubleshooting for "No data is visible" involves resetting SME, using ping, verifying network addresses, checking RCP settings, checking communication settings (protocol, port 502, network adapter, device IP) on KEPServer, and inspecting hardware for malfunctions.
If measured viscosity differs from reference standards, consider if the fluid is non-Newtonian, as readings may vary due to shear rate.
Wrong parameter readings might be due to an incorrect Modbus register address (0/1 indexing) or mismatched data types.
If the sensor status does not match the sensor status table, the sensor status byte might be swapped.
A constant density value displayed on the SRV indicates it is a constant value set with the RCP software.
Rheonics provides online documentation for SRV inline viscometers, SRD inline density meters, electronics and communication, and Modbus TCP input registers.
All listed online resources were accessed on November 27, 2024.
warnings_notes: When no data is visible, troubleshoot by: resetting SME/restarting devices, pinging KEPServer/SME, verifying no duplicated network addresses, checking RCP settings, confirming KEPServer communication settings (protocol, port 502, network adapter, device IP), and looking for hardware malfunctions (broken cable, bad Ethernet Port).

If measured values differ from reference standards for non-Newtonian fluids, understand that viscosity readings may vary due to shear rate; consult Rheonics' guide on Non-Newtonian fluid viscosity measurement with inline viscometer SRV.
For wrong parameter readings, verify the Modbus register address (check 0/1 indexing) and ensure the data type matches the expected configuration.
If the sensor status does not match the sensor status table, check if the sensor status byte is swapped.
If the density value on the SRV is always constant, note that this value is set with the RCP software.
